notes.1: Usually poisoned (Poisons, pp. 115-116), but must penetrate DR to deliver poison.
notes.2: Requires two hands to ready, but only one hand to attack.
notes.3: May entangle or ensnare target; see Special Rules for Thrown Weapons (Exploits, pp. 43-45).
notes.4: A net has no 1/2D Range. Max Range is (ST/2 + Skill/5) for a large net and (ST + Skill/5) for a melee net; round down. A melee net can also be held and used as a melee weapon with reach 1, 2.
notes.5: An atlatl hurls standard throwing darts or javelins; a woomera hurls standard spears. Compare the Thrown Weapon (Dart) and Thrown Weapon (Spear) listings.
notes.6: Dedicated throwing axes and hatchets that lack proper handles cost $10 less, but give -2 to skill as melee weapons (and thus -1 to Parry).
notes.8: In melee combat, small and large throwing knives work, respectively, as small and large knives, but give -2 to skill (and thus -1 to Parry on top of the usual -1 to Parry for knives).
notes.101: Usually poisoned. Add the cost of one dose of poison per dart.
notes.102: Ordinary rocks and stones can be found almost anywhere (in combat, make a Scrounging roll), but are less accurate: -1 to hit.
notes.103: Lead bullets give +1 damage and double range.
notes.201: Can be thrown. See the Ranged Weapons Table (pp. 103-105).
notes.202: May get stuck; see Picks (Exploits, p. 39).
notes.203: Specifically designed to target chinks in armor (Exploits, p. 37). Reduce the penalty for this by -2.
notes.204: Armed or unarmed, this attack receives damage bonuses for whichever of Boxing (p. 93), Brawling (pp. 93-94), or Karate (p. 94) is used to deliver it. Claws, like those of cat folk, don’t affect damage with blows assisted by gauntlets, boots, or weapons.
notes.205: Gives the hand (only) DR 4. A cestus is a glove, mutually exclusive with other gloves. In all other cases, this is a metal hilt; DR is cumulative with that of gloves, but the hilt is too cramped to accommodate metal gauntlets.
notes.206: If you miss with a kick, roll vs. DX to avoid falling.
notes.207: Attempts to parry flails and kusaris are at -4; knives and fencing weapons (“F” parry) can’t parry at all! Attempts to block such weapons are at -2.
notes.208: A rope garrote; a wire one costs $10. See Garrotes (Exploits, p. 39).
notes.209: Gets +2 to disarm when wielded with this skill; see Attacking Weapons (Exploits, p. 37).
notes.210: Improved disarm on your turn after a parry; see Swords (p. 81).
notes.211: Use Boxing, Brawling, or Karate parry if better than weapon parry.
notes.212: Tridents are awkward and always have -2 to hit, but there’s no additional -2 for attacking to disarm; see Attacking Weapons (Exploits, p. 37). The many points give the target -1 to Dodge, +1 to Block or Parry.
notes.213: The butt of a dueling glaive, dueling halberd, naginata, or pollaxe used as a staff. Cost, weight, and ST are as listed for the weapon under the Polearm skill.
notes.214: Fully functional as a digging shovel. Used by grave- digging undead-slayers.
notes.215: Specify maximum reach (1-7 yards) when bought. Cost and weight are per yard. The bullwhip requires ST 3, +1 per yard. The spiked whip requires ST 5, +1 per yard. Many special rules apply; see Whips (Exploits, p. 39).
notes._comment: Weapon Notes start at 1, Amunition Notes start at 101, Melee Weapon Notes start at 201
